source_metadata.title_raw: Sue Novasel - A-18-006 - February 1, 2018 - South Lake Tahoe

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 1, 2018

Sue Novasel
El Dorado County Supervisor
3080 Elf Lane
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-006

Dear Ms. Novasel:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, an El Dorado County Supervisor, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the County's review and potential amendment of its regulations governing short-term rentals of residential properties?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in these decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the general public based on the facts presented.

FACTS

El Dorado County has regulated the use of short-term rentals of residential properties in some areas of the County since 2004. Given the recent increase in the use of residential properties as short-term rentals, the County has established an ad hoc committee to begin policy deliberations on a county wide short-term rental ordinance. Because you represent the area of the County with the greatest number of residential properties used as short-term rentals, the Board selected you to be one of the two Supervisors assigned to the ad hoc committee.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-006
Page No. 2

You and your husband own your residence within the county, your residence has never been
operated as a short-term rental, and you have no intention to do so in the future. Your husband has
ownership interests in four businesses which operate as mortgage lenders and have mortgages on
residential properties located within the County. These interests are:

• A 50-percent stake in Novasel & Schwarte Investments Inc. dba Western Highland
Mortgage Co.
• A 25-percent stake in Novasel & Schwarte Investment Inc. Profit Sharing Plan.
• A 1.365-percent stake in Western Highland Mortgage Fund I, a California LLC.
• A 0.94031-percent stake in Western Highland Fund II, a Nevada LLC.

None of these businesses has control over whether its mortgaged residential properties are
used as short-term rentals. The terms of each mortgage do not require the underlying property to be
used as a primary residence, and no terms of any of the mortgages would change as a result of the
borrower's decision to rent the underlying residential property as a short-term rental. If the County
decides to ban the use of all residential properties as short-term rentals, the ban would not affect the
businesses' existing mortgages or their ability to provide mortgages on single family residences
within the County in the future.

ANALYSIS

Sections 87100 and 87103 prohibit a public official from taking part in a governmental
decision if that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the general public.
Section 87103 identifies the financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the Act. Of those interests, the decisions at issue and the facts presented implicate the
following:

Real Property Interest

An official has an interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) You and your husband own your residence.
Assuming your ownership interest is worth $2,000 or more, you have a real property interest in
your residence.

Business Interests

An official has an interest in any business in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) An "indirect investment interest" means
any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of an official, by an agent on
behalf of an official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agent,
spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or
greater. (Section 87103.) Your husband has ownership interests in each of the four businesses at
issue, each worth substantially more than $2,000. You have an indirect investment of $2,000 or
more in each of those businesses. Therefore, you have a business interest in each of those
businesses.

File No. A-18-006
Page No. 3

Source of Income Interests

An official has an interest in any source of income to the official aggregating $500 or more
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c).) The Act's definition of "income" includes any community property interest in
the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which
the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater
interest. (Section 82030(a).)

Therefore, assuming you have aggregated $500 or more in community property income
from each of your husband's business in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue, you have a
source of income interest in each of those businesses. And if your husband has a 10-percent or
greater interest in such a business, as he does with respect to two of the income businesses at issue
(Novasel & Schwarte Investments Inc. dba Western Highland Mortgage Co. and Novasel &
Schwarte Investment Inc. Profit Sharing Plan), you may also have a source of income interest in a
client of either of those two businesses if your pro-rata share of income from that client exceeds
$500 in the 12 months preceding the decision at issue.

Interest in Personal Finances

An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate
family members. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal
finances.

Therefore, you have a real property interest in your residence, business interests in each of
your husband's four businesses at issue, source of income interests in those businesses and potential
source of income interests in the clients of two of those businesses, and an interest in your personal
finances or those of immediate family members.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

For a financial interest that is not a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental
decision at issue, the financial effect of that decision on an official's financial interest is reasonably
foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) None of your interests are a named party in, or the subject of,
the decisions relating to the potential amendment of the County's regulations governing short-term
rentals. Therefore, the effect of those decisions on each of your interests is reasonably foreseeable if
it is a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Your Residence

Regulation 18702.2(a)(12) provides that a decision's effect on an official's real property
interest is material if a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the

² While an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate
family member (see Section 87103; Regulation 18700(c)(6)), an effect on an official's personal finances is not
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation
18702.5(c)).

File No. A-18-006
Page No. 4

circumstances, would believe that the decision's effect would influence the market value of the
official's property. You and your husband own your residence, your residence has never been
operated as a short-term rental, and you have no intention to do so in the future. There is no
indication from the facts presented that the County's potential amendment of its regulations
governing short-term rentals of residential properties would have any effect on the value of your
residence.

Your Husband's Businesses

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's interest in a business entity and an interest in a business as a source of income.³
Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material
"if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the … value of a privately-held business
entity."

Your husband has ownership interests in four businesses which operate as mortgage lenders
and have mortgages on properties located within the County. None of these businesses has control
over whether any of the mortgaged properties are used as short-term rentals, as the terms of each
mortgage do not require the underlying property to be used as a primary residence. No terms of any
of the mortgages would change as a result of the borrower's decision to rent the underlying property
as a short-term rental. If the County decides to ban the use of all homes as short-term rentals, the
ban would not affect the businesses' existing mortgages or their ability to provide mortgages on
single family residences within the County in the future. Therefore, the decisions at issue would not
contribute to a change in the value of your husband's businesses at issue based on the facts
presented.

Clients of Your Husband's Businesses

In regard to clients of Novasel & Schwarte Investments Inc. dba Western Highland
Mortgage Co. or Novasel & Schwarte Investment Inc. Profit Sharing Plan, you may have a
disqualifying conflict of interest to the extent that there will be a reasonably foreseeable material
effect on the client, the client's business, or the client's property. (See Regulation 18702.3.)
However, under the public generally exception, an official may take part in a decision if the effect
of the decision is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Based upon the facts
provided, the short-term rental ordinance will apply county wide. Under these circumstances, the
potential effect on the clients, who have borrowed funds under the identified mortgages, is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally because the ordinance will apply to all
residential properties and there is no unique effect on the clients. (See Regulation 18703.)
Accordingly, you are not disqualified from the decisions at issue based upon the potential effect on
these clients.⁴ Note, however, that we do not analyze the potential effect on any other clients that
have not been identified.

³ See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).

⁴ If the scope of the decisions at issue is narrowed so that the decisions would no longer apply County wide,
you should seek additional advice regarding the applicability of the public generally exception.

File No. A-18-006
Page No. 5

Thus, we conclude that the decisions relating to the County's potential amendment of its
regulations governing short-term rentals would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on your financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the general public based on the
facts presented.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you, an El Dorado County Supervisor, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the County's review and potential amendment of its regulations 
governing short-tenn rentals of residential properties?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in these decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your financial interests 
distinguishable from the effect on the general public based on the facts presented.
